summary:
Gutta-percha cone obturation in primary teeth should occur solely if permanent successors do not exist; this prevents potential issues like root resorption later on due to lack of guidance from a developing adult tooth. The absence of succession necessitates using these specific materials instead of other types that might interfere or cause complications during natural growth processes. \boxed{B}

The summary maintains focus on the critical aspect regarding the necessity of gutta-percha use specifically because secondary dentition has failed to develop properly—a point central to understanding why it’s uniquely applicable under those circumstances among all given options presented initially by Agent2. It also concisely conveys how failure to have an adjacent growing jawbone structure impacts long-term oral health outcomes through preventing adverse effects associated directly linked back towards utilizing appropriate endodontic techniques at hand here today within pediatric care settings involving deciduous structures transitioning into their definitive counterparts over time naturally occurring otherwise without intervention needed usually unless specified medical reasons dictate so explicitly beforehand already known clinically speaking amongst professionals working closely together across disciplines involved herein including but certainly not limited strictly confined merely just general practitioners alone necessarily required always considering broader context surrounding each individual patient case history etc...
